title: Linked Lists
description: A linear data structure where elements are not stored at contiguous memory locations. Instead, elements (nodes) are linked using pointers.

sections.title: Nodes and Pointers
sections.content: Unlike an array, a linked list does not provide instant access by index. Each element is an independent object called a 'Node'. A Node contains two pieces of information: the actual data, and a 'Pointer' (a reference or memory address) to the next Node in the sequence. The list ends when a pointer points to 'null'.

sections.title: Dynamic Memory Allocation
sections.content: Because nodes are linked by pointers, they can be scattered anywhere in the computer's memory. This means a Linked List can easily grow or shrink in size during execution without needing to pre-allocate massive blocks of memory, making insertions and deletions highly efficient.

sections.title: Real-World Application
sections.content: Linked Lists are heavily used in operating systems and software architecture. Common examples include implementing 'Undo/Redo' functionality in text editors, managing browser history (Previous/Next pages), and building foundational structures like Stacks and Queues.
